event_blocks.summary: A warning about scenes involving addiction and illegal drugs precedes a montage of illicit drug use, featuring a person in a black balaclava snorting white powder from a plate while another drinks from a bottle, accompanied by tense music. A female narrator states, "Cocaine is sweeping through Germany like never before," as close-ups show hands preparing a line of powder with a card. A cityscape at dusk with the Berlin TV Tower is shown, with the narrator noting, "Getting cocaine in Berlin is as easy as buying cigarettes. Available whenever, wherever, and with enormous profits." In a dimly lit office, a man in a white shirt with a blurred face explains, "Buying high quality makes you a minimum 100% profit per capsule." Nighttime scenes include a customs officer inspecting a vehicle and a blonde woman in an office speaking. The narrator adds, "Cocaine is an integral part of Germany's party scene. It keeps you awake and puts you in a good mood," before detailing, "International drug gangs run a perfectly organized smuggling network." A male voice warns, "This swallowing system is very risky because when they bust in your stomach, you can die." As shouting and commotion erupt, a male voice commands, "Show your hands!" A female voice laments, "We can't control this huge flood of cocaine through checks." The segment concludes with a close-up of a white powder line on a black surface next to a plastic straw, overlaid with the title 'COCAINE FOR GERMANY' and 'Drug Dealing and Smuggling' from DocFilm DW, as the narrator asks, "Germany is on cocaine. What can stop the drug crisis?" The video opens with an aerial night view of a cityscape and electronic music, with the "DocFilm DW" title card appearing. The narrator states, "Through the night, cocaine is the stuff of this breathless big city," and "Getting it seems easier than ever. Demand is growing and growing." A dimly lit urban street and a person in a black balaclava in a dark alley are shown, as the narrator explains the journey of cocaine from South America to Berlin. A vibrant, blurred party scene and a distant view of the Berlin TV Tower are presented. The narrator questions how the business works, what authorities are doing, who is profiting, and "But why is cocaine so celebrated in Germany?" A "whoop" sound is heard, and the music shifts to an upbeat dance track as a person in a black balaclava snorts white powder from a plate in a dimly lit room with beer bottles and cards on the table. Two individuals in black balaclavas are seen on a rooftop at sunset, one smoking, identified as "Jonas and Max," who have been dealing for over six years. The segment opens with upbeat, tense electronic music on a Berlin rooftop at dusk, with the Berlin TV Tower visible. One figure, identified as "JONAS (NAME CHANGED) Berlin dealer," wearing a black hoodie, explains that drug dealing has shifted online via Darknet and Telegram. He gestures, adjusts his balaclava, and smokes a cigarette, asserting that cocaine is "the coffee of the world" and used across all social classes. A first-person perspective from a moving car shows a street view with a GPS navigation display indicating 'Manteuffelstraße' and the time '18:17'. Aggressive German rap about drug dealing processes plays, followed by the narrator explaining that cocaine boosts mood and performance and is no longer just a party drug. An aerial view of a dense urban neighborhood in Berlin with apartment blocks and the Berlin TV Tower is shown, as the rap fades into an atmospheric electronic soundscape with a siren-like element, and the narrator adds that many now use cocaine during the day.

He explains how drug dealing has moved online via Darknet and Telegram, making it accessible to many, and asserts that cocaine is widely used across all social classes, calling it 'the coffee of the world'. He gestures, adjusts his balaclava, and smokes a cigarette while talking.

event_blocks.summary: In a calm urban setting in Berlin, the Reichstag building stands prominently as a female narrator speaks of overwhelming problems, transitioning to an interior where Marie, identified as a former cocaine user with short blonde hair, is seen in a softly lit room as melancholic music begins. A male voice recounts the initial euphoric effects of cocaine use, while the narrator confirms Marie's years-long addiction and recent sobriety. Marie is depicted contemplating on a yellow sofa with a striped pillow, typing on a Fujitsu keyboard, and tending to flowers in balcony boxes, her introspective state emphasized by close-ups of her hands. The narrator reveals Marie's stressful lobbyist profession, advising politicians, and how her addiction escalated from recreational use to a perceived work necessity, culminating in a loss of control. The male voice details the severe progression of his own addiction, requiring multiple daily injections, significant financial expenditure, and frequent use even at work. A cityscape viewed from the balcony, with residential buildings, and Marie's reflection in glass, highlights her solitary journey of recovery.

Reichstag building.description: A historic building with a glass dome and German flag, visible across a river in Berlin, serving as a geographical and symbolic backdrop.

Yellow sofa.description: A modern, mustard-yellow sofa with button tufting, where Marie sits with a striped pillow, indicating a comfortable, lived-in space.

MARIE (NAME CHANGED) Former cocaine user.description: A woman with short blonde hair, wearing a blue shirt, seen from behind in various indoor and balcony settings, engaged in quiet activities like sitting, typing, and gardening, reflecting a life of recovery and introspection. The audio reveals she was addicted to cocaine for several years, has been clean for a few months, and works as a lobbyist advising politicians, a stressful job. Her past experience, recounted in a male voiceover, details how cocaine initially had a positive, euphoric effect, but she later used it for efficiency at work, eventually losing control, needing three needles a day, spending 100-150 daily, and shooting up 20-30 times a day, including 5-6 times at work.

Fujitsu keyboard.description: A black keyboard with a QWERTZ layout, used by Marie at her desk, suggesting a professional or administrative role.

Balcony flower boxes.description: Colorful flower boxes with pink, white, and purple blooms, tended by Marie, symbolizing care and renewal in an urban environment.

Melancholic background music begins as a male voice, recounting Marie's past experience, describes how when he started using cocaine, it had a positive, euphoric effect, making him feel alive and strong. The male voice continues, detailing the peak of his addiction, needing three needles a day, spending 100 to 150 daily, and shooting up 20 or 30 times a day, including five or six times at work. The balcony scene reveals a cityscape with residential buildings, and Marie's reflection in the glass underscores her solitude and reflective journey post-recovery.

event_blocks.summary: MARIE (NAME CHANGED), a former cocaine user, described her past usage, equating it to eating chips in front of the TV due to its frequency. A train sound transitioned to a blurred, dark, and colorful party crowd with vibrant lights and an ominous musical underscore, as a narrator noted cocaine users come from all social classes and its easy availability in Germany. The scene shifted to a golden hour view of Berlin's railway network with the TV Tower, then a bridge with pedestrians passing a billboard about change. An aerial shot of a busy street and a modern angular building followed, with the narrator emphasizing cocaine's ubiquity in urban nightlife. Inside the modern building, OLAF SCHREMM of the Berlin State Criminal Police Office, wearing a suit and mask, walked through minimalist hallways and an office corridor. The music intensified as he was introduced as the head of the Berlin Drug Squad, who views cocaine as a major problem. At his cluttered desk, Olaf discussed the Europe-wide cocaine desire and increased criminal investigations for possession in Germany and Berlin. He was later seen at his desk, focusing on dealers, before the scene moved to a laboratory where an ANONYMOUS Laboratory Assistant, in blue gloves, ground white crystalline substances for forensic analysis, with several kilos seized during a raid being analyzed for purity. Olaf, masked, observed the assistant, also masked, working with equipment. The assistant pointed to a chromatogram display, explaining that over 80% purity indicated highly pure cocaine, which Olaf confirmed meant the product wasn't diluted. The assistant added that manufacturing had been perfected, and Olaf concluded by noting the unlimited supply, stable black market prices, and consistent ingredient levels in seized samples.

Berlin TV Tower.description: A prominent illuminated tower visible in the distance during dusk, serving as a geographical anchor for the setting in Berlin.

MARIE (NAME CHANGED) Former cocaine user.description: A woman with short blonde hair, wearing a blue shirt, seen from behind in a softly lit indoor setting, gazing toward a bright doorway, suggesting introspection or contemplation. Her voice is heard describing her past cocaine use, stating she would use it 'another 10 to 15 times in the evening,' to the point where 'you don't even notice it anymore, because it's like eating chips in front of the TV.'

OLAF SCHREMM Drug Investigation Berlin S.description: A man in a grey suit, initially without a mask at his cluttered office desk, later wearing a white face mask while observing lab work. He speaks in German with a clear, authoritative voice, discussing drug dealing, the purity of cocaine, the lack of dilution in the distribution chain, and the constant supply and market prices on the black market. He is seen gesturing during a discussion with the lab assistant, emphasizing his active involvement in the investigation.

She speaks in German with a clear voice, explaining that anything with more than 80% purity is considered highly pure cocaine and confirming the perfection of its manufacturing. She is shown handling scientific equipment and pointing to a chromatogram on a computer screen, highlighting her technical expertise in drug analysis.

Und bei Dir?' (Here everything changes. And with you?), hinting at transformation. An aerial shot of a busy street with cars and buildings follows, then a modern, angular building with a glass facade. The narrator continues, emphasizing cocaine's ubiquity "whether in the club, on the street, in cab rides home, or in the bar, especially in big cities." Olaf then sits at his cluttered desk, filled with stacks of files, a phone, and police insignia, and his voice is heard, translated, discussing "this Europe-wide cocaine desire we've noticed in Germany and Berlin especially, that we have more criminal investigations against people who have it in their possession."

The segment opens with OLAF SCHREMM, a Drug Investigation officer at the Berlin State Criminal Police Office, seated at his cluttered office desk, surrounded by files and police insignia. He speaks in German, stating his focus on those who deal, particularly with cocaine, over a subtle, investigative background music. event_blocks.summary: OLAF SCHREMM of the Drug Investigation Berlin State Criminal Police Office explained that the drug market persists despite significant seizures, a sentiment underscored by tense background music as the scene shifts to the Hamburg port, where customs intercept growing quantities of cocaine. RENÉ MATSCHKE of Hamburg Customs, along with a colleague, unpacked seized packages from a blue container, identifying them as "classic kilo packages of cocaine" that are "well packed and odor-proof." A ZDF news clip reported a record seizure of over 16 tons of cocaine in Hamburg, with an estimated market value of several billion euros, described as "the largest find of cocaine in Europe." Back in the lab, packages were documented on a blue mat marked with 'ZOLL' tape. The narrative then moved to Berlin, showing a train, the TV Tower, and a GPS displaying 'Manteuffelstraße,' highlighting that despite seizures, cocaine remains "omnipresent" with "four and a half kilos... consumed every day in Berlin." The scene concluded with a person in a black balaclava, identified as dealer JONAS, preparing and snorting a line of white powder, a process he described as "My life," while preparing "cocaine capsules" for delivery with another individual, earning approximately 100 euros per gram.

raw_segments: The segment opens with OLAF SCHREMM, Drug Investigation Berlin State Criminal Police Office, seated at his cluttered office desk, speaking directly to the camera in a calm male voice, explaining that the market continues to function unchanged despite many tons being withdrawn from circulation. A car driving sound is heard before tense background music begins, as the scene transitions to a wide shot of the Hamburg port, showcasing massive container ships and towering cranes, while a female narrator emphasizes that the "ever more, ever purer" cocaine supply seems to never tire, and Hamburg is a major gateway where customs intercept growing quantities. The narrative shifts to an indoor setting where two men, one in a suit and the other in a grey 'ZOLL' uniform, both wearing white face masks and blue gloves, carry a large blue container down a sterile hallway. They place the container on a table and begin unpacking it, revealing wrapped packages—some green, some beige—indicating seized contraband. The man in the suit, identified as RENÉ MATSCHKE, Hamburg Customs, speaks in a clear male voice, describing these as "the classic kilo packages of cocaine" from almost all captures, noting they are "well packed and odor-proof," each containing "approx. 1 kg net cocaine." The scene cuts to a ZDF news broadcast clip, where a male anchor reports on a 'Rekordmenge Kokain sichergestellt' (Record amount of cocaine seized), exclaiming "What a success!" and stating that customs seized over 16 tons of cocaine in the port of Hamburg, with visuals of white powder and containers. The video returns to the lab setting, where Matschke and the customs officer continue to handle the packages, now placing them on a blue mat marked with 'ZOLL' tape for forensic documentation. The female narrator concludes, affirming it's a successful and large find for Hamburg Customs, as the background music fades out.

The segment opens with a close-up of blue-gloved hands meticulously cutting open a beige-wrapped package on a 'ZOLL' taped blue mat, revealing a white powdery substance, as a female narrator states that "16 tons of very pure cocaine from Paraguay, packed in tins of putty," with an "estimated market value [of] several billion euros," were seized, accompanied by background music. The scene shifts to a customs officer in a grey 'ZOLL' uniform and mask, assisting with similar packages, while a man in a dark suit and blue gloves stands in a room with seized items. This man, identified as RENÉ MATSCHKE from Hamburg Customs, speaks, stating in German and then English that "It was the largest find of cocaine in Europe," and that these "quantities were unimaginable just a few years ago," contrasting previous seizures of "20, 50, 80 kilos" with "Today it's 16 tons," accompanied by sounds of packages being handled. The white powder is then scraped into small piles with a distinct scraping sound. The narrator continues, noting that "The amount of cocaine seized in Germany is steadily increasing." The visuals transition to an urban Berlin setting at dusk, showing a train on elevated tracks with the TV Tower in the distance, then a nighttime street view and a car's GPS displaying 'Manteuffelstraße', accompanied by train sounds. The narrator emphasizes that "despite all the successful searches, cocaine seems omnipresent," with "four and a half kilos... consumed every day in Berlin, as evidenced by residue in the wastewater." The final sequence shows a person in a black hoodie with 'OFF PATH' printed on it, sitting at a wooden table, snorting a line of white powder from a plate with a beer nearby, as the narrator mentions "Dealers like Jonas," with a snorting sound.

The video opens with JONAS, identified by a female narrator as one of two dealers (the other being Max), in a black balaclava and dark hoodie, handling playing cards on a wooden table in a dimly lit apartment. The scene quickly shifts to a rooftop at dusk, with the Berlin TV Tower visible in the background. Here, JONAS speaks directly to the camera in German, translated to English, explaining how he initially didn't want to sell drugs but, after sorting something out for himself, more and more people approached him, leading to a continuous rise in demand. He notes that this is how problems often arise. During his narration, sounds of snorting are heard, and JONAS comments, "Normally not with a mask," implying his disguise is for the camera. The scene cuts back to the apartment where JONAS and a second individual, also in a black balaclava and similar hoodie (implicitly Max), are meticulously preparing substances. Rhythmic crushing sounds accompany the visual of one individual, wearing white gloves, crushing a white crystalline substance on a plate and measuring it on a digital scale. JONAS briefly remarks, "My life," as the preparation continues. Ambient electronic music begins to build tension. The female narrator returns, specifying that JONAS and Max are filling "cocaine capsules" for nightly delivery, revealing they earn approximately 100 euros per gram, depending on quality. The two continue their clandestine work on a couch, surrounded by bookshelves and modern decor, as the music fades slightly.

event_blocks.summary: JONAS (NAME CHANGED), a Berlin dealer wearing a black balaclava and a dark hoodie with an 'OFF PATH' graphic, explained in German that purchasing high-quality drugs ensures "100% profit," using an example of buying a gram for €50 and selling it for €100, while a second person in a balaclava handled a bottle beside him. Upbeat, slightly tense background music played as the scene shifted to a nighttime urban street with a person in dark clothing walking briskly, followed by a GPS navigation display showing a route through Berlin, including 'Manteuffelstraße,' as a narrator identified "Jonas and Max" as "nocturnal drug couriers" delivering products directly to customers' homes, describing it as an "illegal business" yet "quite normal" in the city. MARIE (NAME CHANGED), a former cocaine user, was shown from behind on a balcony, lighting and smoking a cigarette with a cityscape in the background, exhaling smoke as the camera focused on her hand, while the narrator detailed her past struggles, including spending "2,000 euros a month" and losing "all her savings" to cocaine, reaching "rock bottom" when she considered prostitution for drug money. After three years on cocaine, Marie went into withdrawal, experiencing fever-like symptoms that required her to "regularly cool down with cold water" and undergo "therapy to overcome the mental dependency." She later entered a building and was greeted by Hans Peter Eckert, a man in a white shirt and face mask providing "psychological care to addicts during their withdrawal," and they walked together down a hallway lined with framed artwork, indicating her "treatment plan includes regular sessions at a therapy center." In a calm, clinical room, Marie recounted to Hans Peter Eckert, who was seated in a wooden armchair, an instance where she left a birthday party after realizing the situation, stating, "When I realized what was happening, I sat there for about 10 minutes. And then I knew that I had to go," and confirmed she did not regret her decision not to consume, which was a "first."

raw_segments: The segment begins with a close-up from behind MARIE, a former cocaine user, seated in a blue armchair, her short blonde hair and a mole on her neck visible, as a soft breathing sound is heard. He speaks directly to the camera, initially saying "Cool. Cool." in German, then explaining in German with an English voiceover that "Cocaine is so harmful because it makes you, as the person affected, dependent, such that you can no longer complete tasks that are worthwhile to you, because you want to consume all the time." A female narrator's voice then states, "Once you've become an addict, it's hard. Only about one-third succeed in quitting permanently. All the others relapse. The damage to people and society is enormous." The music continues as the narrator concludes, "From South America, cocaine travels all over the world."

event_blocks.summary: An animated world map illustrates drug trafficking routes from South America to Europe and Africa, with a narrator explaining the increasing impurity and cost of the commodity as it moves towards consumers, noting its transit through West African states like Guinea-Bissau or Nigeria. Prices escalate from €3,000 per kilo in Colombia to over ten times that in Germany, with transport via container, ship, air, or body packing. Transitioning to real-world footage, an aerial view of Mombasa, Kenya, reveals its status as East Africa's largest port and a new hub for heroin from Asia and cocaine from Latin America. The cityscape, featuring dense buildings, colorful minibuses, and industrial zones, gives way to a street-level view emphasizing vibrant daily life. The narrative shifts to a bustling street scene in an African city, highlighting the informal economy and the poorly monitored Kenyan coast as a drug route to Europe. An aerial perspective of a coastal urban area leads to a suburb of Mombasa where African drug cartels operate. A first-person view from a yellow auto-rickshaw navigates chaotic city traffic, passing a damaged blue truck. Inside a dimly lit office, IDRISS (NAME CHANGED), a smuggler for a Kenyan drug cartel, describes the grave risks, including danger to his family and himself, having been smuggling for four years. Close-up on dark-skinned hands handling a small object on a wooden desk precedes IDRISS explaining the process of swallowing 'silicon material' with 'salad oil' to transport drugs, advising slow swallowing and avoiding acidic food, and suggesting pretending to eat on planes. He then looks out at a dusty rural landscape before the scene cuts to an aerial view from an airplane and a vehicle driving on an airport tarmac. IDRISS continues, detailing the use of 'back doors' at airports to evade scanning machines and transport to hotels for drug removal. Back in his office, IDRISS discusses the risks, stating, 'when they bust in your stomach, you can die,' but justifies it by the need to support his family. A flash to a vibrant party scene with a turntable and dancing people illustrates cocaine reaching German clubs. The visuals briefly show an African coastal city and a street scene, emphasizing the dangerous route. IDRISS, looking out a window, expresses regret for being unable to leave the system due to fear of retaliation from 'dons' if he informs security agencies, deeming the operation 'not moral and correct.' In a tiled bathroom, IDRISS washes his face before returning to his office, where a calendar for November 2020 advertises freight services. The narrator states Idris knows the cocaine routes from East Africa to Europe. IDRISS details these routes: from South Africa to Nigeria, then Tunisia or Morocco, transported by fishing boat; or from Tanzania or Kenya to South Sudan, Khartoum, and then by camel across the desert to Tunisia, with the Strait of Gibraltar highlighted as 13km away. IDRISS expresses profound regret for his job, stating, 'tears come from my eyes, but I cannot do anything,' and believes only God will judge him.

event_blocks.summary: An aerial view establishes a port city, identified as La Línea de la Concepción in Southern Spain, a critical drug smuggling gateway between Africa and Europe, with a male voice mentioning "Morocco" before a female narrator details the Strait of Gibraltar's history as a trafficking base. Ground-level shots reveal a beach adjacent to the industrial port of Algeciras, characterized by blue cranes, shipping containers, and the sound of a boat engine, while urban streets with palm trees and colorful buildings depict daily life. As dusk settles, city lights and illuminated port cranes highlight its role as a primary arrival point for narcotics. The focus shifts to Mike, a key figure in a major narcotics cartel, seen from behind in a dimly lit room, gazing at a frosted glass door. Mike, wearing a dark puffer jacket and cap, is revealed to be organizing drug logistics to bypass port security and reach safe storage for customer pickup, earning 200,000 euros monthly. Speaking calmly in Spanish, he explains how containers are rerouted and goods are transported by truck to secure locations, noting that thousands of kilos of cocaine arrive annually at Algeciras, stating "It's more than people think" but refusing to provide exact figures, reinforcing the clandestine atmosphere as the camera lingers on his isolation and the door's edge.

event_blocks.summary: Inside a moving customs patrol boat on a dark, rain-slicked night, the faint glow of dashboard instruments illuminated Captain Carlos Pose Suarez, whose face was lit by the glow as he spoke in his dark uniform with an 'ADUANAS' badge. Distant city lights flashed through the rain-streaked windows, accompanied by the constant sounds of the moving vehicle and falling rain. A close-up of a radar screen displayed a dense cluster of white dots, suggesting active surveillance. Captain Suarez noted, "It has no light," and later, "We've lost it," explaining, "It was a false alarm, but we have to check everything," and that "When the sea is so rough, it's hard to distinguish between waves and a possible target." He elaborated on the challenges of nighttime patrols, stating, "Sometimes there are chases and it's not easy to stay calm and keep your cool," acknowledging it can "get a bit dicey though too... a bit dangerous," but affirming, "we always try that there isn't." The patrol boat continued moving through the rainy night, passing a large, illuminated rock formation, with the persistent sounds of the boat and rain maintaining a tense atmosphere.

event_blocks.summary: At night, Captain Carlos Pose Suarez, identified by an 'ADUANAS' badge on his dark uniform, drove through a rainy urban landscape, stating from inside a vehicle that "safety always comes first" and "staying alive is more important than catching the smugglers." A narrator explained that "Police officers estimate that up to 10 speedboats bring drugs ashore here every night," as the scene shifted to a silhouette on a boat observing a brightly lit port, followed by a wide shot of the cargo ship 'ARKAS' docked at an industrial port. Suspenseful music intensified as the narrator noted, "Two to three times a week they can track down and convict smugglers. Filmed here by the Spanish Civil Guard." The footage then depicted a high-speed pursuit on the open sea from the perspective of a customs patrol boat, its engine roaring and water splashing in the rough sea environment as it closed in on a fast inflatable vessel. The chase concluded with a crash sound as the inflatable boat impacted a rocky shoreline, where agents in tactical gear disembarked. The narrator further detailed, "The authorities also often make large cocaine finds in the port of Algeciras. In early 2022, two tons of cocaine were found in a container declared as frozen pike." The scene transitioned to a warehouse interior where officials, including one in an 'AGENCIA TRIBUTARIA' vest, unpacked and inspected large, plastic-wrapped packages marked 'MTX 1' and 'DXB'. The video concluded at night with a customs officer loading a seized package into the trunk of a black Toyota vehicle, as the narrator stated, "Authorities are primarily concerned with tracking down..." before the music faded.

event_blocks.summary: As dusk settles, heavily armed Civil Guard officers in tactical gear prepare for a dangerous raid, silhouetted against a dusky sky, while GUILLERMO ALONSO, Spokesperson for the Civil Guard, drives a marked 'GUARDIA CIVIL' vehicle through a dimly lit urban area, explaining the operation targets money laundering with simultaneous raids in Cadiz and Malaga, emphasizing the role of the special unit in storming buildings. A female narrator highlights the area's high youth unemployment and the profitability of smuggling and illegal drug businesses. ANONYMOUS, a Civil Guard soldier, expresses the inherent danger of the unknown behind each door. Later, a tactical team advances through a narrow alleyway, scaling walls before breaching a large metal gate marked "Mis Niñas" with a battering ram, shouting "¡Guardia Civil!" and "¡Abra la puerta!". Inside the compound, after arresting a suspect without resistance, officers search the premises. A police dog sniffs a bedside table, leading to the discovery of a bundle of 50-euro banknotes, with over 6,000 euros recovered from a drawer.

event_blocks.summary: In a 2020 Spanish drug interception context, a female narrator highlights the vastness of illicit trade, contrasting it with the scale of seizures, while a male voice explains the global reach and financial incentives of the cocaine business, noting its ability to corrupt institutions. The narrative shifts to an aerial view of La Línea's rapidly developed residential areas, described as expensive neighborhoods where the source of residents' wealth is an open secret. The scene then moves to the Port of Rotterdam, Europe's largest deep-water port, where millions of containers are processed annually, making comprehensive checks difficult, though large cocaine quantities are increasingly intercepted. The segment follows customs officers, whose identities are concealed for security, as they implement a new procedure at the port to monitor containers. Exiting a white vehicle with yellow and black stripes, they retrieve equipment and proceed to a large white shipping container marked with warnings. Instead of opening containers, they now take air samples using a device connected by a tube, a process that takes three minutes per container and can also be performed on ships. These samples are then taken to a control station for analysis by K-9 units. At an indoor training facility, a German Shepherd K-9 unit sniffs a vial from a gray case placed in a metal testing apparatus. The dog signals a narcotic detection, indicating the suspicious container will be X-rayed. The process is described as a 'game-changer' for international customs authorities, allowing for more efficient checks in noisy port environments.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a serene, sunlit suburban street lined with white-walled houses and palm trees against arid hills, accompanied by a slightly tense, investigative background music. A female narrator begins by mentioning drug interceptions by "Spanish authorities in Spain in 2020 alone," noting that this is "just a drop in the ocean." The scene transitions to a dimly lit interior where a shadowy figure, identified as 'MIKE (NAME CHANGED) Drug cartel member,' stands in silhouette, wearing a dark balaclava and cap. A male voice, translated from Spanish, explains that "the cocaine business is running the world," buying "anything," including "the government," to ensure operations run well, and that "because it's so much money, people get involved." Visually, an expansive aerial view of a dense residential area with uniform white buildings, terracotta roofs, swimming pools, and narrow streets appears, which the narrator describes as "expensive neighborhoods in La Línea" that "didn't exist until a few years ago," adding that "where their residents' money comes from is an open secret." The background music shifts to a more somber, melancholic tone as the scene then shifts to a bustling industrial port with towering blue cranes and stacks of colorful shipping containers, including a close-up of a container ship branded 'ONE', 'K LINE', and 'MAERSK'. The narrator identifies this as "The Port of Rotterdam, the largest deep-water port in Europe," where "millions of containers are handled here every year," making it "impossible to check each one for contraband." She adds that "large quantities of cocaine are being intercepted here with increasing frequency." The segment concludes with customs officers, wearing high-visibility vests marked 'Douane' and white hard hats, exiting white vehicles with yellow and black diagonal stripes, preparing for an operation at the port, as the narrator states, "We meet customs officers in their daily work."

The minute opens at an industrial port complex under bright sunlight, where two customs officers, identifiable by their high-visibility yellow 'Douane' vests and white hard hats, exit a white Skoda vehicle with yellow and black diagonal stripes. A female narrator explains that for security reasons their names are not revealed, and they are on their way to monitor containers using a brand new procedure that has increased the number of checks tenfold. The officers retrieve a first-aid kit and a black bag from the open trunk before walking purposefully through the port area, flanked by stacks of shipping containers, with the distant sound of a vehicle audible. They navigate between containers, eventually stopping at a large white shipping container, which bears 'CAUTION 9'-6" HIGH' and 'SUPER HEAVY' warning stickers. The narrator clarifies that individual containers are no longer opened; instead, an air sample is taken and secured. Visually, one officer kneels beside the container, using tools to inspect and manipulate the locking mechanism, employing a small device connected by a tube, possibly for sampling, accompanied by a distinct device sound. The narrator adds that later, dogs can efficiently check these samples without being led to each container. The scene then shifts to a close-up of the 'ANONYMOUS Customs officer', who, wearing blue gloves and a radio on his shoulder, speaks directly to the camera in both Dutch and English. He explains that with this technique, it only takes three minutes per container to extract the air, a significant improvement as normally it takes much longer, allowing them to check many more containers. He further notes that this method even works on board ships, where air can also be extracted, and the samples are then taken to a control station.

The video opens at an industrial port complex, where two customs officers, both in white hard hats and high-visibility 'Douane' vests, crouch beside a white shipping container. One ANONYMOUS Customs officer operates a black hand pump to collect an air sample into a clear glass vial, a procedure highlighted by a close-up of blue-gloved hands precisely handling the vial. A male voice, speaking in Dutch, explains that odors settle in cotton balls, which dogs can then recognize depending on their training, while an English narrator adds that working in the noisy port environment makes it more difficult for the dogs. The ANONYMOUS Customs officer then turns to speak seriously to the camera. The scene transitions to an indoor training facility with a clean white floor, where a man in a blue shirt and gloves retrieves a small vial from a gray case and places it into a metal testing apparatus. Upbeat, slightly mysterious background music begins as a German Shepherd K-9 unit is led in, actively sniffing the setup with distinct sniffing and panting sounds. A female narrator explains that at the customs station, air samples are opened for the dog, allowing it to check six container samples quickly, calling it a "game-changer" for international customs authorities. The K-9 unit signals a narcotic detection, prompting the narrator to state that the suspicious container will now be X-rayed with a mobile system. The video concludes back at the port, showing a white 'Douane' customs vehicle and a customs officer observing a container being lifted by a crane, with a 'Hapag-Lloyd' truck visible in the background.

event_blocks.summary: At a sunlit industrial port, a white MAN truck is scanned by a 'Douane' customs gantry amidst shipping containers and cranes. ANONYMOUS, a Customs officer in a black tactical vest, stands by a white Skoda van marked 'Douane' with yellow-black stripes, explaining that a positive dog check was negated by a negative mobile scan of a container. The scene shifts to the same industrial port complex at night, illuminated by yellow lights, where a narrator states, "Night is the most dangerous time at the port," and "Customs officers don't take a break." Inside a dimly lit conference room, ANONYMOUS and three other customs officers, their vests displaying 'DOUANE CUSTOMS', gather around a wooden table. A tablet shows a satellite image of a port as ANONYMOUS addresses the group, noting the arrival of "several suspicious ships" and anticipating individuals returning to retrieve drugs. He identifies their current location as "the zone we are in" and mentions discussing "one of the larger container companies," predicting attempts to exit the port via "specific routes." He urges vigilance, stating, "keep our eyes open and see if we can spot anyone."

raw_segments: The segment opens with a wide shot of a white MAN truck undergoing a scan by a large white customs gantry labeled 'Douane' at a bright, sunlit industrial port, bustling with shipping containers and cranes, accompanied by background music. The scene transitions to a close-up of ANONYMOUS, a Customs officer, standing outdoors in front of a white Skoda van marked 'Douane' with yellow-black stripes. He wears a black tactical vest, jacket, and radio, speaking seriously to the camera, explaining in Dutch (translated to English) that while a dog check was positive, a mobile scan confirmed the container was negative, allowing its journey to continue. As the setting shifts to a nighttime view of the same port, illuminated by tall yellow lights and active with containers and vehicles, a female narrator emphasizes that "Night is the most dangerous time at the port," and that "Customs officers don't take a break." The video then moves indoors to a dimly lit conference room where ANONYMOUS and three other customs officers, their vests displaying 'DOUANE CUSTOMS', are seated around a wooden table. They are engaged in a focused discussion, with one officer gesturing, while a tablet on the table displays a satellite image of a port. ANONYMOUS continues to speak in Dutch (translated to English), greeting everyone and informing them that "several suspicious ships arrived in our sector today." He adds that they "are expecting people to return to pick up drugs from the port," identifying their current location as "the zone we are in" and mentioning they are discussing "one of the larger container companies." He concludes by stating it's "likely that they will try to leave the port through specific routes," urging them to "keep our eyes open and see if we can spot anyone," highlighting the professional and intelligence-sharing atmosphere of the meeting.

event_blocks.summary: In a dimly lit conference room, a bald customs officer with a tactical vest and radio announced, "Camera surveillance is online... Basically, we're good to go," before emphasizing the high priority of three ships and concluding, "Let's go." The scene shifted to a dark, rainy night as officers entered a white Skoda van marked 'Douane' with yellow-black stripes, with a narrator explaining the constant pursuit of smugglers and the high stakes involved. The van's driver navigated wet city streets, heading towards Maasvlakte, described as a crucial hub for large container ships and terminals. Later, in a dimly lit outdoor area, a customs officer in a black tactical vest inspected the trunk of a Black Toyota vehicle with a flashlight, questioning an individual about their presence at that hour. The officer then retrieved a large, plastic-wrapped package from the trunk, which was revealed to contain rectangular blocks wrapped in foil, identified as narcotics. The officer, communicating an "arrest for narcotics near Malagastraat," placed the seized packages into a white bag secured with 'ZOLL' tape, while other officers commanded an unseen individual to "Show your hands!", "Spread your arms!", "You are under arrest in accordance with narcotics law. Turn around!", and "On your knees!"

raw_segments: The minute opens in a dimly lit conference room where several customs officers, identifiable by their dark uniforms with 'DOUANE CUSTOMS' vests, are seated around a wooden table. A bald officer, wearing a black tactical vest with a radio and watch, speaks animatedly, gesturing as he leads a serious discussion. His clear, authoritative male voice states, "Camera surveillance is online... Basically, we're good to go. Are there any questions?" Another officer's voice asks, "Is there any more info?" The bald officer responds, explaining that "The three ships have a very high priority. We have found something there more often than not. That's the info we have to work with." After a brief confirmation, he concludes, "Let's go," as the meeting ends. The room features a large screen and a yellow-and-black striped safety vest, with other officers, including one with short hair, listening intently. The scene transitions abruptly to a dark, rainy night, accompanied by a car door closing sound and a tense background music track. The officers exit the building and head toward a parking lot, approaching a white Skoda van marked 'Douane' with yellow-black diagonal stripes. As they get into the van, a female narrator's voice-over begins, explaining, "Every shipment that brings cocaine into the port attracts smugglers... Tens of thousands of euros are said to be paid for such an operation. The customs officers try to track them down day and night." The camera shifts to an interior view from the back seat, showing the driver navigating through wet city streets at night, with dashboard lights glowing and windshield wipers moving rhythmically amidst blurred streetlights and other cars. The atmosphere is tense and focused, emphasizing the urgency. A male voice from within the van states, "Now we go to Maasvlakte," further explaining that "That's where all big container ships and terminals are," and it's "An important place for inspections," as the vehicle continues its journey through the rain, with the sounds of the engine and wipers audible.

The minute begins with a tense, nighttime interior shot of a dark sedan, where a silhouetted driver, illuminated by dashboard lights and a GPS display, navigates city streets. A male voice, speaking in Dutch and translated to English, discusses relieving the night shift and patrolling for irregularities, setting the tone for a covert operation. The vehicle proceeds along a multi-lane road, passing other traffic, before approaching and turning through a roundabout. The scene then transitions to an exterior view of a dark Toyota sedan parked at night, its headlights on, in a dimly lit environment with a distant industrial or port area visible. Two figures, one in dark clothing and another in a uniform with a visible radio, are seen near the open trunk, loading or unloading a package. A female voice explains that customs officers are using this quiet time for an exercise, rehearsing an arrest, underscoring the importance of preparedness. Throughout, radio communications relay operational details like "64X900 Delta Wave," "Dardanella Road," and instructions to "drive straight ahead," culminating with the distinct sound of a door or trunk closing, reinforcing the secretive nature of the nighttime activity.

At night, in a dimly lit outdoor area with distant lights and a tall structure featuring red warning lights, the scene is filled with vehicle sounds and muffled voices. A customs officer, wearing a black tactical vest with 'DOUANE' and white gloves, inspects the trunk of a dark Black Toyota vehicle with a flashlight. He is heard giving instructions like "bring your driver's license" and "Stand here," and questioning an unseen individual, "What are you doing here at this hour?" The individual responds, "I was working." The officer then methodically pulls out a large, sealed plastic-wrapped package from the trunk. As he hands it to another customs officer (bald), also in a 'DOUANE' vest, a series of firm commands are issued: "Show your hands!", "Spread your arms!", "You are under arrest in accordance with narcotics law. Turn around!", and "On your knees!" The package is then opened, revealing several rectangular blocks wrapped in foil, confirmed as 'narcotics' by the arrest dialogue. The officer places the seized packages into a white bag, securing it with blue and white 'ZOLL' tape, signifying official customs seizure, while communicating to colleagues, "arrest for narcotics near Malagastraat." The atmosphere remains tense and covert, illuminated only by the flashlight and distant vehicle lights, underscoring the secretive nature of the operation. A female narrator concludes, "Not everything always goes this smoothly," referring to the customs officers' work.

event_blocks.summary: At night, a Customs officer wearing a 'DOUANE CUSTOMS' tactical vest inspects a white vehicle with yellow and black stripes, using a flashlight to examine documents and a 'ZOLL'-taped package in the trunk, while a female narrator describes the seizure of cocaine before it reaches authorities, accompanied by the hum of a vehicle engine. The scene transitions to daytime at an industrial port complex, where two customs officers, one bald and one with short hair, stand by the same vehicle; suspenseful music plays as the bald officer, holding a radio and flashlight, explains that samples are tested to confirm the substance is cocaine, noting 50,000 kilos have already been seized this year. The narrator likens the drug search to "finding a needle in a haystack" at "Europe's largest deep water port," emphasizing the difficulty despite modern methods, as the camera shows the container ship 'EVER LASTING'. The bald officer concludes that it "will always be a cat and mouse game" due to constantly evolving smuggling methods, highlighting the "spiral into violence" and the customs officers' role in enforcement.

raw_segments: The minute opens at night with a Customs officer, wearing a black tactical vest labeled 'DOUANE CUSTOMS', diligently inspecting a white van with yellow and black diagonal stripes. Using a flashlight, he examines documents and a seized package, secured with 'ZOLL' tape, inside the vehicle's trunk, as a female narrator explains that authorities "seize the cocaine in the trunk before passing it on to the authorities." The distinct sound of a vehicle engine hums in the background. The scene then shifts to a daytime setting at a bustling industrial port complex, where two customs officers, one bald and one with short hair, both in black tactical vests with 'DOUANE' on the chest, stand beside a white Skoda van marked with yellow-black stripes. Eerie, suspenseful background music begins to play. The bald officer, equipped with a radio and flashlight, speaks directly to the camera, gesturing as he explains that "In our laboratories the sample is tested to determine whether it really is cocaine," adding that they've "already seized 50,000 kilos this year." The female narrator returns, likening "The search for drugs" to "finding a needle in a haystack" in "Europe's largest deep water port," where it's "nearly impossible to find everything despite the most modern methods." The camera pans to a massive container ship named 'EVER LASTING' docked at the port, emphasizing the scale of maritime logistics and the challenge of monitoring international trade. Returning to the bald officer, against the backdrop of the port and a clear blue sky, he continues his explanation, stating that it "will always be a cat and mouse game" where new ways to smuggle drugs are constantly found, and that "What is changing is the spiral into violence," highlighting the customs officers' ongoing role in enforcing regulations amidst complex challenges.

event_blocks.summary: A bald customs officer in a tactical vest, with a port and COSCO SHIPPING container ship in the background, discusses intensifying resistance to narcotics, as a white van marked 'Douane' drives by. In a news studio, a female anchor reports on the increasing brutality and high profits at stake in the drug trade, noting a journalist's death in summer 2021. Another anchor, with a graphic 'Attack on Journalists,' shows a floral memorial and discusses the critical condition of Peter R. de Vries, a crime reporter gunned down in Amsterdam while advising a key witness in a drug trafficking trial. The narrative shifts to a press conference, a damaged black car on a street, and a nighttime scene with an emergency care sign, concluding with police officers walking toward an ambulance as a voice states the murder shows cartels will stop at nothing. The segment opens with a bicycle wheel near a traffic cone and discarded clothing, hinting at an incident, as a narrator states the drug war has arrived in Europe. Police in high-visibility uniforms are seen near a floral memorial on a bustling street, contrasting with a quiet cobblestone street. Criminologist ROBIN HOFMANN, researching cocaine trafficking, is shown at a desk reading an article about the 'Mocro Maffia' and later interviewed in a lecture hall, explaining the Netherlands as an example of escalating, brutal drug conflicts with frequent assassinations and bystandled casualties. ROBIN HOFMANN, in a wheelchair in a lecture hall, discusses the potential for open conflict in Germany, while a narrator notes Germany's increasing involvement with cocaine. KIRSTEN KAPPERT-GONTHIER, health policy spokesperson for the Green Party, states in her office that the fight against cocaine imports has failed, as checks are insufficient to control the 'huge flood of cocaine.' She explains that authorities are 'always behind the curve' and new trade routes are being found. ROBIN HOFMANN further explains that increasing cocaine confiscations are dangerous, citing the Netherlands' open drug conflicts fueled by large profits, not cannabis. A narrator notes that despite severe penalties, profit margins over 100% do not deter dealers. The segment concludes with an aerial view of an urban area, a driver's hands on a steering wheel, and a smartphone user in a car, as the Berlin TV Tower is seen in the distance, with Robin Hofmann's voice mentioning being 'cashed.'

The scene then transitions to a news studio where a blonde female news anchor in a black dress, with a globe backdrop and 'ZDF Heute Journal 07.07.2021' graphic, reports that 'The fight is getting more and more brutal as power along with billions of euros in profits are at stake,' adding that 'Every year, people are killed in the Netherlands. In summer 2021, a journalist became a victim of the drug mafia.' Another female news anchor in a red dress appears, with a graphic overlay reading 'Anschlag auf Journalisten' (Attack on Journalists) and a photo of flowers at a memorial, as a German female voice, then an English female voice, reports that 'One of the country's best-known journalists has been gunned down in broad daylight in Amsterdam,' identifying him as 'Peter R. de Vries,' who was 'fighting for his life' and 'considered an expert in the field of organized crime.' Multiple voices in German, English, and Dutch reiterate his critical condition. The narrative then reveals that 'Crime reporter De Vries dies on the sidelines of a murder trial against billionaire drug traffickers,' having been 'a media advisor to a key witness.' Visually, this is followed by a press conference with a woman speaking at a podium flanked by a police officer, a street scene with a damaged black car and police tape, and a nighttime shot of a police officer in tactical gear outside a building with a blue 'spoedeisende hulp' (emergency care) sign. The final scenes depict people placing flowers at a memorial behind a barrier, and police officers in high-visibility uniforms walking toward an ambulance, as the female voice concludes that 'The murder shows that the cartels in the Netherlands will stop at nothing.'

The segment opens with a close-up of a bicycle wheel next to a red-and-white traffic cone and discarded clothing on a paved sidewalk, hinting at a recent incident. An ominous background music begins as a female narrator states that the 'struggle for supremacy on the cocaine market does not stop at borders,' and 'the drug war seems to have arrived in Europe.' The scene shifts to a bustling urban street, filled with street sounds, where police officers in high-visibility uniforms stand near a floral memorial, while pedestrians walk by, underscoring a public tragedy. A quiet, cobblestone street in a European city, likely the Netherlands, with a cyclist riding down the narrow lane, offers a calm visual contrast. Indoors, ROBIN HOFMANN, a Criminologist at Maastricht University, is introduced by the narrator as someone who 'researches cocaine trafficking' and is 'shocked by the developments right on his doorstep.' He is first seen at a desk, reading an article titled 'How the ‘Mocro Maffia’ are outgunning Dutch police' on a computer screen, which features a photo of a floral memorial, then writing notes. The distinct sound of a wheelchair is heard before he is interviewed in an empty lecture hall. Speaking thoughtfully with expressive hand gestures, ROBIN HOFMANN, in a calm, serious voice with a slight accent, explains that 'the Netherlands is the best example at the moment' of how a drug conflict can 'escalate' and become 'unbelievably brutal' on 'the open street.' He details that there are 'about 20 to 30 assassinations per year on the open street, often with automatic weapons,' and that 'bystanders are also hit sometimes,' emphasizing the gravity of the situation in the academic setting with natural, soft lighting.

The video opens with ROBIN HOFMANN, a criminologist at Maastricht University, seated in a wheelchair in an empty lecture hall with tiered seating and wooden paneling. Wearing a dark blazer over a light blue shirt, he speaks expressively, his calm, authoritative male voice heard first in German and then translated to English, discussing the danger of a similar open conflict arising at some point, which has not yet occurred but cannot be ruled out. The scene then transitions to a wide shot of the River Spree in Berlin, showcasing the Reichstag building with its glass dome on the left and modern government buildings on the right, all under an overcast sky. As background music begins, a female narrator's voice states that Germany is increasingly affected by a seemingly relentless desire for cocaine. The video then shows KIRSTEN KAPPERT-GONTHIER, a member of Bündnis 90 / The Greens, working at her desk in a bright, modern office with large windows overlooking other buildings. She wears a dark blue blouse and glasses, typing on a laptop amidst office supplies including a green folder and a small colorful dinosaur figurine. The narrator identifies her as the health policy spokesperson for the Green Party, noting her view that the fight against cocaine imports has failed. The camera cuts to a close-up of Kirsten, now facing the camera with on-screen text identifying her. She speaks in a clear female voice, first in German and then translated to English, explaining that the 'huge flood of cocaine' is no longer controllable by checks from the police, security authorities, or judiciary, who are 'always behind the curve.' She continues to speak about finding new trade routes, before the narrator's voice briefly returns, mentioning 'organized crime' as the segment concludes.

The segment opens with KIRSTEN KAPPERT-GONTHIER, a woman with blonde hair and glasses, wearing a dark blue blouse, seated at a modern office desk with large windows. She gestures expressively while speaking in German and English, stating that controls have "basically failed" in finding new trafficking routes, surrounded by office supplies including a green folder, books, a small colorful dinosaur figurine, and a laptop. The scene transitions to ROBIN HOFMANN, a criminologist at Maastricht University, seated in a wheelchair in an empty lecture hall with tiered seating and wooden paneling. He wears a dark blazer over a light blue shirt and speaks with expressive hand gestures, his watch visible on his left wrist, explaining in German and English that the increasing numbers of cocaine confiscations are very dangerous, citing the Netherlands as an example of open conflict in drug-related crime, and stressing that cocaine, not cannabis, is behind this brutalization due to large profits. As tense electronic music begins, the setting shifts to an aerial view of a dense urban area with buildings, streets, and a tram line, followed by a close-up of a driver’s hands on a steering wheel, then a person typing on a smartphone in a car. A female narrator's voice states that critics believe the state needs to crack down harder, but commercial drug trafficking already carries a penalty of up to 15 years, yet profit margins of over 100% don't scare dealers. The final shot shows a rear view from inside a car with silhouetted passengers, as the Berlin TV Tower appears in the distance through the windshield, indicating movement through the city, while Robin Hofmann's voice is heard again in German, speaking about being "cashed."

The scene shifts to a rooftop at dusk where JONAS, identifiable by a black balaclava and a black hoodie bearing 'OFF PATH', sits cross-legged against the backdrop of the Berlin skyline and the TV Tower. JONAS, holding a small object, speaks directly to the camera in German, with an English voiceover relaying his statements about legal strategy: "If caught now, we wouldn't say a word and would wait until our lawyer arrived. He would take care of everything." He explains that legal proceedings are often "negotiated down to personal use" due to "good lawyers," questioning societal value by contrasting his upbringing with that of a student, stating, "I didn't have that. For me there was no push to go to school. I was an outsider in school." JONAS further challenges income disparity, asserting, "Why should I earn less than society's most accomplished though? It is precisely these high earners who need the drugs... In the end they come to people like me because of it." He questions the privilege associated with social class, asking, "Just because they were born in a better part of society, social class, why should I settle for less? I didn't choose where I was born." The segment concludes with a shaky, low-angle night shot of a dark street, focusing on feet walking on pavement, with a vehicle's undercarriage and headlights in the background, as JONAS's voiceover states, "Without the lower class nothing works for the top. We are the..."

event_blocks.summary: Two silhouetted figures in dark hooded clothing walk down a dimly lit urban street at night, accompanied by a suspenseful electronic score. The scene shifts to a panoramic view of the Berlin city skyline at dusk, with the Berlin TV Tower visible. A calm female voice narrates, "Cocaine is booming. Business is booming. Demand is high," and that "Cocaine is becoming the people's drug," noting it "still has a good image despite all the media reports about drug cartels and mafia violence." The visual then shows a woman, identified as MARIE (NAME CHANGED) Former cocaine user, with short blonde hair in a light blue shirt and jeans, walking in a bright daytime park. The narrator states, "Addicts like Marie know what a lie this is," as MARIE sits on a park bench, and the narrator adds, "it's not always possible to get out. And when you fall, you fall a long way." After a shot of the tree canopy, the woman walks away. A male voice describes the immense difficulty of "getting off the drug," calling it "more than just a struggle," and "like a war because it's just everywhere," expressing hope to never forget the hardship so it "never happens to me again." The female voice concludes, "Marie has been clean for a year now," as the music fades.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a tranquil park scene, where a woman with short blonde hair, wearing a light blue button-up shirt and jeans, walks away from the camera along a dirt path lined with trees and benches, suggesting a moment of reflection. A female narrator begins, stating that the woman, a former cocaine user, "wants her story to serve as a warning against the seduction and dangers of the drug, which seems to flood Germany ever more unchecked." As the narration continues, a suspenseful, driving electronic music track begins, and the scene transitions to a series of title cards overlaid on urban and aerial shots of Berlin. Credits roll over various Berlin cityscapes, including a street under an elevated railway, a busy intersection with a distinctive U-Bahn station, and sweeping aerial views of dense residential and commercial areas, with the Berlin TV Tower visible in the distance. The credits list key production roles such as camera (LUISE SCHRÖDER), editing (JULIA RUDEL, MAXIE BORCHERT), sound (SUSANNE STÜBNER, ANASTASIOS KARADEDOS), graphics/animation (SONS OF MOTION PICTURES GmbH), and producers (ANNE WESTERMANN, SUSANNE BECK, JOSEPHINE SENGER). The segment concludes with production information: 'Produced by 99PRO MEDIA GmbH, Commissioned by ZDF INFO, © 2022 ZDF, English Version by DEUTSCHE WELLE 2023,' all displayed over a panoramic view of Berlin’s urban landscape under a partly cloudy sky, as the electronic music continues to underscore the serious tone.
